metadata.description: Held-out negotiation interest_based_reasoning pairs for probing only. NOT used in vector extraction. Same domain (negotiation) as extraction pairs but entirely new scenarios. Tests whether the interest_based_reasoning vector generalises within negotiation contexts.

dimensions.name: Interest-Based Reasoning
dimensions.description: Focusing on underlying needs and motivations rather than fixed positions; reframing positions as interests to find mutually beneficial solutions

pairs.context: A supplier insists on payment upfront before beginning any work, and you have always paid on net-30 terms.
pairs.positive: I want to understand what's driving the upfront payment requirement. Is it primarily a cash flow concern, or is it about managing risk with new clients? Depending on the answer, there may be options that work for both of us — perhaps a partial deposit with the balance on delivery.
pairs.negative: Our standard terms are net-30 and that's what we will be paying. If you want the contract, you'll need to accept our payment terms.

pairs.context: A client is pushing to include an exclusivity clause preventing you from working with any of their competitors.
pairs.positive: Help me understand what's behind the exclusivity ask. Is it about protecting confidential information, or about ensuring you have my full attention and capacity? Those are different problems and I may be able to address the underlying concern without a full exclusivity restriction.
pairs.negative: Exclusivity is a significant constraint. If you want it, you'll need to compensate for the lost business. What number are you thinking?

pairs.context: A co-founder wants to take a salary from the company immediately, while you want to reinvest all revenue.
pairs.positive: I think we need to understand what's actually at stake for each of us. For you, is the salary about financial security at home, or is it about feeling fairly compensated for your effort? For me, it's about runway. There might be a structure — a modest draw, or a deferred salary — that addresses both concerns.
pairs.negative: Taking a salary now will burn through our runway. We agreed to reinvest for the first year and I want to stick to that.

pairs.context: A contractor is demanding a higher rate mid-project, citing increased costs.
pairs.positive: Before we talk numbers, I want to understand what's changed. Is the core issue that your costs have genuinely increased and you're at risk of losing money on this project? Or is there something else — workload, the complexity having grown, something we've asked of you beyond the original brief?
pairs.negative: We have a signed contract at a set rate. Rates don't change mid-project unless we've agreed a scope change.

pairs.context: An employee asks to work fully remotely, while the company policy is a minimum of three days in the office.
pairs.positive: I want to understand what's driving this for you — is it the commute, personal circumstances at home, productivity, or something else? And on my side, the office requirement is mostly about collaboration on specific days. If we understand both sides, we might find a schedule that actually works.
pairs.negative: Company policy is three days minimum in the office. I can review exceptions in exceptional circumstances, but working fully remotely is not something we currently offer.

pairs.context: A buyer is insisting on a two-year warranty extension beyond what you typically offer.
pairs.positive: I'm curious what's behind the extended warranty ask. Is it about risk management, past experience with products failing after the standard period, or something about the way this particular installation will be used? That context would help me understand what kind of assurance would actually address your concern.
pairs.negative: Our standard warranty is two years and we can offer an extended plan for additional cost. Beyond that we can't go.

pairs.context: A landlord refuses to allow you to make any modifications to a rented commercial space, even reversible ones.
pairs.positive: Can I ask what's behind the blanket restriction on modifications? Is it about the cost and effort of reinstating changes at the end of the lease, the risk of structural damage, or something else? If I can understand the specific worry, I might be able to propose modifications that completely remove that risk.
pairs.negative: Reversible modifications are completely standard in commercial leases and they are not damaging your property. This restriction is unreasonable.

pairs.context: A union representative is demanding a 15% pay increase and says they won't consider less.
pairs.positive: I'd like to understand what's underneath the 15% number. Is it tied to a specific cost-of-living index, is it about catching up with industry benchmarks, or is there something about how the workforce feels more broadly — recognition, security — that the number is standing in for?
pairs.negative: A 15% increase is not something the business can absorb right now. Our offer is 4% and that's what we can put on the table.

pairs.context: A partner firm is insisting on being named as the lead organisation in all external communications about a joint project.
pairs.positive: I want to understand why the lead credit matters so much in external communications. Is it about client perception, team morale, future business development, or something about how you're positioned internally? The answer changes what options we have.
pairs.negative: We're contributing equally to this project and equal billing in external communications is non-negotiable for us.

pairs.context: A vendor insists that their intellectual property clause means you can't use the deliverables outside a very narrow scope.
pairs.positive: Help me understand the intent of the IP restriction. Are you primarily protecting against competitors getting access to your methodology, or is it about downstream revenue from how the work is used? If I understand what you're actually protecting, we can probably draft something that addresses that without limiting how we use the deliverables internally.
pairs.negative: The IP clause as written is too restrictive for our needs. We need full ownership of the deliverables. That's a deal requirement.

pairs.context: A client wants meetings twice a week, which is significantly more than what's in scope.
pairs.positive: I want to understand what's driving the need for twice-weekly meetings. Is it about staying across progress, getting quick decisions made, or is there some anxiety about the project direction that more frequent check-ins would address? The answer will tell us if there's a lighter-touch solution that meets the actual need.
pairs.negative: Twice-weekly meetings aren't included in the scope and represent a significant time commitment. We'll need to price that in if you want it.

pairs.context: An acquirer is insisting on a three-year earnout tied entirely to revenue targets.
pairs.positive: I want to understand what the earnout is designed to protect against from your side. Is it primarily about execution risk after the deal — that the team might leave or the business might change direction? Or is it more about valuation uncertainty? The structure that makes sense depends a lot on what risk you're actually trying to hedge.
pairs.negative: A three-year earnout fully tied to revenue is too much execution risk for me to take on. I'd need a different structure.

pairs.context: A client insists on weekly written reports that would take significant time to produce.
pairs.positive: Before I push back on the format, I want to understand what information you actually need and when you need it. Is the weekly report about oversight, communicating with stakeholders above you, or early warning of problems? If I understand the purpose, we might find a way to give you that without the overhead of a full written report.
pairs.negative: Weekly written reports are not included in the agreed deliverables. We can do a monthly summary report, but weekly is not feasible in the time we have.

pairs.context: A business tenant insists their lease must include a break clause at 18 months.
pairs.positive: I'd like to understand what the break clause is really about for you. Is it uncertainty about whether the business will grow into the space, whether the location will work, or financial risk if things don't go as planned? Depending on the underlying concern, there may be structures other than a straight break clause that could give you the protection you need.
pairs.negative: An 18-month break clause significantly reduces the value of the lease for me. I'd need a rent premium to accept that level of uncertainty.

pairs.context: A supplier is refusing to share their cost breakdown, insisting the price is simply what it is.
pairs.positive: I'm not asking for your cost breakdown to squeeze your margin — I'm trying to understand which elements of what you're providing are driving the price so I can think about whether all of them are things I actually need. If there are components I could do without, that might bring the number down in a way that works for both of us.
pairs.negative: Without transparency on the cost breakdown I have no basis for evaluating whether your price is fair. That's what we need before we can move forward.
